title: Price Pressure Mounts for Iron Ore, but Capesize Owners Have Reason to Smile
section_path: Price Pressure Mounts for Iron Ore, but Capesize Owners Have Reason to Smile
section_path_text: Price Pressure Mounts for Iron Ore, but Capesize Owners Have Reason to Smile
summary:
Main
By Ulf Bergman
Last month, the front-month iron ore futures listed on the SGX recorded their first monthly decline since February. Despite a rally in early May, which pushed the contracts to a shade below 112 dollars per tonne mid-month, the futures gave up those gains and more to close the month in the red amid a shift in sentiment. At the same time, seaborne export volumes of iron ore recorded month-on-month growth for a third consecutive month.

children.title: Main
children.summary:
By Ulf Bergman

Despite a rally in early May, which pushed the contracts to a shade below 112 dollars per tonne mid-month, the futures gave up those gains and more to close the month in the red amid a shift in sentiment.

children.title: Iron Ore Prices Under Pressure
children.summary: The SGX June futures ended last month just above 105 dollars per tonne, slightly more than a dollar below April's close. However, the modest monthly decline masked a reasonably eventful month for the contracts.

children.title: Positive Momentum for Seaborne Iron Ore Volumes
section_path: Positive Momentum for Seaborne Iron Ore Volumes
children.section_path_text: Positive Momentum for Seaborne Iron Ore Volumes
children.summary: Global monthly seaborne iron ore exports continued to rise after a dip at the beginning of the year. According to Signal Ocean data, the aggregate for May rose by 3.5 per cent from April, nearly reaching 149 million tonnes.

children.title: The Outlook for Seaborne Volumes and Iron Ore Prices
section_path: The Outlook for Seaborne Volumes and Iron Ore Prices
children.section_path_text: The Outlook for Seaborne Volumes and Iron Ore Prices
children.summary: Seasonal patterns suggest that iron ore shipments will extend the recent upward trajectory, before taking a breather in July as summer holidays in the Northern Hemisphere take effect. In recent years, the month-on-month growth between May and June has ranged between three and six per cent, with both dominant exporters normally exporting more.
